titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.name: University of South Carolina Center for Digital Humanities
The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive Research and Development Team
University of South Carolina Center for Digital Humanities

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

div.p: Colonel J. Colton Lynes, of the Agricultural College of South Georgia, who is now seeking to obtain some consular or diplomatic appointment, has already had considerable experience abroad as translator and bookkeeper of the United States Legation at Paris. All the correspondence between Bismarck, Washburne and Thiers was translated by Colonel Lynes and transcribed into a volume now in the archives in Washington.

Mr. Van Leer Polk, of Nashville, Tenn., who will soon sail for Calcutta, where he will represent the United States Government as Consul-General, says that he means to give up his home style of dress and drink, and to live like the natives. He thinks this is the secret of escaping cholera and other diseases in foreign lands.

Carl Luther, a counsellor in the German Ministry of Labor, who died a few days ago in Schoenebeck, Germany, was a direct descendant of Jacob Luther, the youngest brother of Martin Luther. His father, August Christian Valentin Gottleib Luther, bore a striking resemblance to the great leader of the Reformation in Germany. A young brother of the dead man was killed in the battle of St. Privat. Direct descendants of Martin Luther, through his son, Dr. Paul Luther, body physician of the Elector of Saxony, still live in Germany. Among them is Deacon Paul Luther, of Kremmen. The family in each generation has usually had several members in the church. A number of the female descendants of the reformer have married into the families of the nobility.

The Rev. D. F. Bradley, pastor of the Park Congregational Church of Grand Rapids, Mich., was born at Bangkok, Siam, in 1857, of missionary parents. He remained in Siam until 1877, acquiring among other things, a knowledge of the Siamese language. He is a graduate of Oberlin College, Ohio.

The Virginia press is urgently recommending the appointment of Senator John W. Daniel, of that State, to succeed Justice Blatchford on the Supreme Bench. Senator Daniel is a close friend of Senator David B. Hill, and an ardent advocate of free coinage, neither of which qualifications is likely to be of much weight with President Cleveland.

Captain Oscar G. Lenge, who has just died at his home in Chicago, was the first Swede to settle in that city. He also, in 1838, opened the first drug store anywhere in that neighborhood. Captain Lenge obtained his title from his marine experience. He was for a period connected with the United States and English navies, and subsequently was engaged in the merchant marine service just previous to his coming to Chicago.

Captain Milton T. Russel, of Des Moines, Iowa, has just published the reminiscences of his prison life in and escape from Libby Prison during the war. He was captain of Company A, Fifty-first Indiana Infantry, and was captured with Colonel Streight's command, near Rome, Ga., during that fearless and famous raid into the heart of the Southern Confederacy.

A recent earthquake at San Nicholas Island, near Santa Barbara, Cal., has revived interest in the story of the old Indian woman who led a Crusoe life on the island for many years. She was left there by accident in 1836, and her existence forgotten until twenty years later, when a Santa Barbara hunter, who had visited the island for otters, found her there and brought her to the mainland. She had entirely forgotten her language, and in consequence the story of her hermitage was never known. Three months after her rescue she died, and was buried by the mission fathers.

Mr. Henry O'Meara, of Boston, who was the poet at the recent dedication of the Farragut statue in that city, has been invited to contribute a hymn for the parliament of religions to be held at the World's Fair.
